does: This paper introduces RWKU, a benchmark for evaluating machine unlearning in large language models, designed to measure how effectively models can forget sensitive or copyrighted knowledge. The benchmark centers on 200 real-world famous people as unlearning targets and operates under a practical constraint where neither the forget corpus nor retain corpus is accessible during the unlearning process. The evaluation framework includes a forget set assessed through four membership inference attack methods and nine types of adversarial attack probes to rigorously test whether unlearned knowledge is truly erased, alongside a retain set that measures whether the model's general capabilities are preserved across various real-world applications after unlearning.
connects: This work builds on the emerging field of machine unlearning for LLMs, which addresses the critical problem of removing memorized sensitive, copyrighted, and harmful information from trained models. It extends prior work on model unlearning by introducing a more realistic and challenging setting—the scenario where neither forget nor retain data are available during the unlearning process, which is more practical than previous benchmarks assuming full access to training data. The paper connects to research on membership inference attacks and adversarial robustness evaluation, adapting these techniques from privacy and security literature to assess unlearning effectiveness. It also responds to the broader movement toward responsible AI and addressing data privacy concerns in large-scale language model deployment.
enables: This benchmark enables systematic evaluation and comparison of unlearning algorithms under realistic conditions, facilitating the development of more effective unlearning techniques for deployed LLMs. The RWKU framework provides researchers with standardized evaluation methods including membership inference and adversarial probes that can be applied to assess future unlearning approaches. By establishing that popular knowledge is widely memorized across different LLMs, the work highlights the scale of the unlearning challenge and points toward future research on efficient knowledge removal without access to original training data. The benchmark framework could support development of privacy-preserving deployment strategies and inform regulatory compliance for handling copyrighted or sensitive material in large language models.
